title: SOC 2 CC6 — Encryption in Transit Policy Enforcement for Admin and API Paths

compliance.assurance_rationale: SOC 2 CC6.6 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that SOC 2 CC6.6 (Encryption in transit) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.8.18: SOC 2 CC6 — Encryption in Transit Policy Enforcement for Admin and API Paths.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.8.18' running on index network and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

app: Splunk Add-on for Zeek (Splunkbase 1617), Splunk Add-on for Nginx or F5 LTM (as applicable)

index=network sourcetype="zeek:ssl" earliest=-24h
| where ssl_version IN ("TLSv1","TLSv1.0","SSLv3") OR like(cipher,"%NULL%") OR like(cipher,"%EXPORT%")
| stats count by server_name, ssl_version, cipher
| sort - count

description: CC6 expects encryption of data in transmission. Detecting TLS policy regressions—weak cipher suites, certificate expirations, or plaintext admin sessions—provides continuous evidence that cryptographic controls remain effective.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for SOC 2 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes network and sourcetypes nginx:access, zeek:ssl. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for CC6 — Encryption in Transit Policy Enforcement for Admin and API Paths. SOC 2 is how we show we run our services responsibly, and we keep evidence that supports that story.
implementation: (1) Expand to internal services behind load balancers using `server_name`; (2) join cert inventory for `cert_expires` within 30 days; (3) separate customer-facing vs admin VIPs; (4) integrate with vulnerability SLA for weak TLS; (5) document compensating controls for legacy apps with approved exceptions lookup. Indexes required: `index=network`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=nginx:access`, `sourcetype=zeek:ssl`.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Zeek (Splunkbase 1617), Splunk Add-on for Nginx or F5 LTM (as applicable).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=network` `sourcetype IN ("zeek:ssl","nginx:access")` (server_name, ssl_version, cipher, cert_expires); tag admin paths via `uri_path`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Expand to internal services behind load balancers using `server_name`; (2) join cert inventory for `cert_expires` within 30 days; (3) separate customer-facing vs admin VIPs; (4) integrate with vulnerability SLA for weak TLS; (5) document compensating controls for legacy apps with approved exceptions lookup.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=network sourcetype="zeek:ssl" earliest=-24h
| where ssl_version IN ("TLSv1","TLSv1.0","SSLv3") OR like(cipher,"%NULL%") OR like(cipher,"%EXPORT%")
| stats count by server_name, ssl_version, cipher
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**SOC 2 CC6 — Encryption in Transit Policy Enforcement for Admin and API Paths** — CC6 expects encryption of data in transmission. Detecting TLS policy regressions—weak cipher suites, certificate expirations, or plaintext admin sessions—provides continuous evidence that cryptographic controls remain effective.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=network` `sourcetype IN ("zeek:ssl","nginx:access")` (server_name, ssl_version, cipher, cert_expires); tag admin paths via `uri_path`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Zeek (Splunkbase 1617), Splunk Add-on for Nginx or F5 LTM (as applicable). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: network; **sourcetype**: zeek:ssl. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=network, sourcetype="zeek:ssl", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Filters the current rows with `where ssl_version IN ("TLSv1","TLSv1.0","SSLv3") OR like(cipher,"%NULL%") OR like(cipher,"%EXPORT%")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by server_name, ssl_version, cipher** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with the GRC system, audit register, or framework crosswalk. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (weak sessions), Line chart (trend of weak cipher hits), Single value (distinct servers).
visualization: Table (weak sessions), Line chart (trend of weak cipher hits), Single value (distinct servers).
knownFalsePositives: Evidence freshness lags during quarterly cycles, control owner transitions, or platform changes.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.action, Authentication.user, Authentication.src span=1d | sort - count
